result:
If I had 1000 words to write whatever came to me, they would not form a single argument, but a mosaic. A scatter-plot of human moments against the vast, quiet backdrop of everything else.

I’d start with the texture of things: the cool, reptilian smoothness of a river stone warmed in a pocket; the crumpled geography of a bedsheet at 3 a.m.; the precise, dusty smell of a book cracked open after decades. These are the anchors, the sensory proof we are here.

Then, I’d wander into the realm of unfinished thoughts. The apology you practiced in the shower that will never be spoken. The name of that song—just on the tip of your tongue, a ghost of a melody. The ghost-limb feeling of reaching for a phone to tell someone news, before remembering they are gone. We live as much in these absences as in our presences.

I’d need to acknowledge the small, private heroisms. The deep breath taken before walking into a difficult room. The choice to be kind when being right would be easier. The daily courage of the chronically ill, the grieving, the simply tired, who get up and make the tea anyway. Not every battle has a banner.

A paragraph would have to be devoted to the sky. Not a romantic, postcard sky, but the peculiar, washed-out blue of a Tuesday afternoon in February. The way clouds can look like continents, or thoughts, or nothing at all. The indifferent, glorious sunset that doesn’t care if you’re watching, painting itself in violet and gold over parking lots and palaces alike. It is the ultimate democratic art.

I’d laugh at our shared, ridiculous humanity. The universal dance of trying to carry too many grocery bags at once. The solemn concentration of a child negotiating with a sock. The bizarre, specific comfort of a favorite mug, even with its chip. Our tragedy and our farce are twins.

I’d venture a guess about time—how it isn’t a line but a pool. How the scent of rain on hot pavement can throw you back to being eight years old, bicycle handlebars sticky with sweat, in an instant. How you can sometimes feel the shadow of a future moment, a quiet pull toward a person or a place you don’t yet know. We are bad at measuring this dimension we swim in.

I would not shy away from the dark stitch in the tapestry: the cold fear that wakes you for no reason. The ache of loneliness in a crowded space. The news cycle’s relentless drumbeat of anger and loss. To ignore this is to paint a portrait with no depth.

But then, I’d turn to the counterweights. The silent understanding that passes between two people with a glance. The first raw, sweet crunch of an apple. The absurd, purring contentment of a cat in a sunbeam. Music—how a sequence of vibrations in the air can make your chest feel like it will split open with sorrow or soar with joy. These are not small things. They are the things that quietly insist: *Continue.*

I’d write about the mystery of other minds. That you will never truly, fully know what it is like to be your mother, your friend, the stranger on the bus staring out the window. Every person is a universe with its own laws, its own history of collapsed stars and nascent light. We bump against each other, guessing, hoping to be understood.

And finally, I’d land on the notion of quiet connection. The archaeologist who brushes dust from a pot shard, touching the fingerprint of the artisan who made it a thousand years prior. You, reading these words I am forming now, separated by who knows what distance or time. We are sending signals across the void, saying, in a million different ways, “I was here. I felt this, too.”

My 1000 words would run out here, not with a conclusion, but with an invitation. To look. To really look. At the way light slants through a window. At the face of someone you love when they are sleeping. At your own hands—these marvels of bone and tendon that have held so much.

The world is bursting with stories waiting to be noticed. They are in the rust on the gate, in the grocery list left on the counter, in the steady, patient heartbeat in your own chest. This is not just a place to pass through. It is a place to be read, like a letter, written to you from the strange and beautiful fact of existence itself. The only task, the only real task, is to pay attention.

And perhaps, to begin writing your own 1000 words back.
